Caption: General concepts of order and disorder in cathode materials. (a) Crystal structure of layered LiCoO2. (b) Schematic illustration of α-LiFeO2 structure (DRX material), where Fe3+ and Li+ randomly sit at cationic sites with equal probabilities. (c) Li-Ni mixing in NMC layered material. (d) Two types of octahedral SRO in DRX-structured α-LiFeO2 (oxygen atoms are omitted here for clarity).

Evolution of cation arrangement with increased cation ordering driven by ionic radius. (a) Cation-disorder rock-salt (DRX) structure. (b) Layered LiMO2 structure. (c) Li2MO3 with honeycomb ordering in TM layer.

Typical origins of cation disorder in cathode materials. (a) Crystal structure of LiMO2 with various M3+ radius, adapted with permission from Wu et al[32]. (b) Magnetic frustration and released magnetic frustration caused by Li-Ni mixing. (c) Super-exchange effect. (d) Distortion of TM-centered octahedron in cation disordered Li1.17Ti0.33Fe0.5O2.

Interlayer and intralayer disorder driven by lithium intercalation and deintercalation. (a) Left: Schematic illustration of surface reconstruction of LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2 when cycled after 50 cycles between 3 and 4.5 V and 3–4.8 V. Right: HR-TEM images of LiNi0.5Co0.2Mn0.3O2 after 50 cycles under 3.0–4.5 V (up) and 3.0–4.8 V (down). (b) STEM pattern of Li1.211Mo0.467Cr0.3O2 (LMCO) electrode before cycling and after 1 and 10 cycles. (c) Left: First cycle voltage hysteresis of Li1.2Ni0.13Co0.13Mn0.54O2 and corresponding cation arrangement at pristine, begin of plateau (BOP), full charge (FC) and full discharge (FD) states. Right: ADF-STEM images of Li1.2Ni0.13Co0.13Mn0.54O2 at pristine, begin of plateau (BOP), full charge (FC) and full discharge (FD) during first cycle along the [58] zone axis. (d) Larger voltage hysteresis of honeycomb-ordered Na0.75[Li0.25Mn0.75]O2 compared with ribbon-ordered Na0.6[Li0.2Mn0.8]O2.

Short range order among DRX materials. Central picture: Different types of SRO among DRX. “Structure” panel: Tetrahedral, octahedral and cubic SROs among DRX materials and their structure in reciprocal space. “Characterization" panel: ED and STEM for SRO probing. “Calculation” panel: Reverse Monte Carlo (RMC) simulation workflow. “Property ” panel: Different amount of Li4 clusters and 0-TM network size due to different types of SRO among LMTO and LMZO.

From micro-scale to meso-scale Li+ diffusion. (a) Illustration of microscopic Li+ diffusion pathway in rock-salt-type structure. (b) Li+ hopping barrier when ecounted with different TM ions with different valence at intermediate tetrahedral site. (c) Isolated Li4 clusters, chained Li4 clusters, and compacted Li4 clusters. (d) Meso-scale Li+ diffusion among cathode particles. (e) Probability of 0-TM percolating under different cation mixing extent and lithium content in LixTM2-xO2.

Cycling stability comparison of order and disorder cation arrangements. (a) Cycling stability of layered Li1.2Ni0.13Mn0.54Co0.13O2. (b) Cycling stability of DRX-structured Li1.3Nb0.3Mn0.4O2. (c) In situ XRD (up) and ex situ k 2 -weighted Fourier transform magnitudes of Mn k-edge EXAFS spectra (down) during first charging (1 C), discharging (1D) and second charging (2 C) process respectively of R 3 ̅ m Li0.700[Li0.222Mn0.756]O2. (d) Cycling stability of regular Li2TMO3 (R-Li2TMO3) and intralayer disordered Li2TMO3 (ID-Li2TMO3).

Voltage hysteresis of layered and DRX material. (a) Schematic illustration of increased O 2p orbital due to TM migration. (b) Close relation between TM migtation and voltage hysteresis in LR-NMC. (c) Voltage hysteresis results from non equilibrium F4+-O2- path (cationic redox) rather than equilibrium Fe3+-O(2-n)-/O2 (anionic redox). (d) Voltage hysteresis in layered materials. (ⅰ) Li1.2Ni0.13Co0.13Mn0.54O2. (ⅱ) LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4. (ⅲ) Li2IrO3. (ⅳ) Li2Ru0.75Sn0.25O3. (e) Voltage hysteresis in DRX materials. (ⅰ) Li1.3Ni0.27Ta0.43O2. (ⅱ) Li1.2Ni1/3Ti1/3Mo2/15O2 (LNTMO20). (ⅲ) Li1.3Nb0.43Ni0.27O2, Li1.3Nb0.3Mn0.4O2, Li1.3Nb0.43Co0.27O2， Li1.3Nb0.3Fe0.4O2. (ⅳ) Li4.15Ni0.85WO6.
